title: Cannot use podman inside lxc with nesting enabled
body:
Hi. Im trying to use podman inside an Alpine Linux container but it cannot start any container. I get the following message. As far as I know this should be possible. Docker works and I am trying to migrate from it.

Packages involved:

Host: Proxmox Ve 7.0
lxc:
lxc-pve/stable,now 4.0.9-4 amd64 [instalado, automático]
lxcfs/stable,now 4.0.8-pve2 amd64 [instalado, automático]
pve-lxc-syscalld/stable,now 1.0.0-1 amd64 [instalado, automático]

systemd:
systemd 247 (247.3-6)
+PAM +AUDIT +SELINUX +IMA +APPARMOR +SMACK +SYSVINIT +UTMP +LIBCRYPTSETUP +GCRYPT +GNUTLS +ACL +XZ +LZ4 +ZSTD +SECCOMP +BLKID +ELFUTILS +KMOD +IDN2 -IDN +PCRE2 default-hierarchy=unified




Guest:
Alpine Linux 3.14
openrc (OpenRC [LXC]) 0.43.3.4753eda10f

````

podman run --rm hello-world
WARN[0000] Failed to add conmon to cgroupfs sandbox cgroup: error creating cgroup path /libpod_parent/conmon: write /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.subtree_control: operation not supported 
Error: OCI runtime error: writing file `/sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.subtree_control`: Not supported


``

comments.body: It seems like cgroups are not mounted inside the LXC container. Are you running Podman inside the container as root, or rootless? Is the host CGroups v1 or v2?
I am using the container as root. I am using cgroups v2 as it is the default in this version of proxmoxfor lxc

Ok so you were right there was an openrc script to mount cgroups. I enabled it

rc-update add cgroups 

rebooted and ... nothing. I got a lot of errors. 

When I try:
````
podman run --rm hello-world
WARN[0000] Failed to add conmon to cgroupfs sandbox cgroup: error creating cgroup path /libpod_parent/conmon: write /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.subtree_control: device or resource busy 
Error: OCI runtime error: the requested cgroup controller `pids` is not available





WARN[0000] Failed to add conmon to cgroupfs sandbox cgroup: error creating cgroup path /libpod_parent/conmon: write /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.subtree_control: device or resource busy 
Error: OCI runtime error: the requested cgroup controller `pids` is not available
````
Also I got :


````
/etc/init.d/cgroups restart
mkdir: can't create directory '/sys/fs/cgroup/openrc': File exists
mount: permission denied (are you root?)
/lib/rc/sh/openrc-run.sh: line 38: can't create /sys/fs/cgroup/openrc/notify_on_release: Permission denied
mount: mounting none on /sys/fs/cgroup/unified failed: Resource busy
mkdir: can't create directory '/sys/fs/cgroup/cpuset': File exists
mount: permission denied (are you root?)
mkdir: can't create directory '/sys/fs/cgroup/cpu': File exists
mount: permission denied (are you root?)
mkdir: can't create directory '/sys/fs/cgroup/cpuacct': File exists
mount: permission denied (are you root?)
mkdir: can't create directory '/sys/fs/cgroup/blkio': File exists
mount: permission denied (are you root?)
mkdir: can't create directory '/sys/fs/cgroup/memory': File exists
mount: permission denied (are you root?)
mkdir: can't create directory '/sys/fs/cgroup/devices': File exists
mount: permission denied (are you root?)
mkdir: can't create directory '/sys/fs/cgroup/freezer': File exists
mount: permission denied (are you root?)
mkdir: can't create directory '/sys/fs/cgroup/net_cls': File exists
mount: permission denied (are you root?)
mkdir: can't create directory '/sys/fs/cgroup/perf_event': File exists
mount: permission denied (are you root?)
mkdir: can't create directory '/sys/fs/cgroup/net_prio': File exists
mount: permission denied (are you root?)
mkdir: can't create directory '/sys/fs/cgroup/hugetlb': File exists
mount: permission denied (are you root?)
mkdir: can't create directory '/sys/fs/cgroup/pids': File exists
mount: permission denied (are you root?)
mkdir: can't create directory '/sys/fs/cgroup/rdma': File exists
mount: permission denied (are you root?)
````

So it might be a permission issue BUT, the container with docker has the same problems and it works. 

What I didn't notice is that thee docker service enables the cgroup service inside the Alpine LXC.

There is this post about cgroups and docker: https://wildwolf.name/how-to-run-docker-in-alpine-container-in-lxc-lxd/:

Also the content of the /etc/init.d/cgroups script is


````
#!/sbin/openrc-run
# Copyright (c) 2017 The OpenRC Authors.
# See the Authors file at the top-level directory of this distribution and
# https://github.com/OpenRC/openrc/blob/master/AUTHORS
#
# This file is part of OpenRC. It is subject to the license terms in
# the LICENSE file found in the top-level directory of this
# distribution and at https://github.com/OpenRC/openrc/blob/master/LICENSE
# This file may not be copied, modified, propagated, or distributed
# except according to the terms contained in the LICENSE file.

description="Mount the control groups."

cgroup_opts=nodev,noexec,nosuid

depend()
{
	keyword -docker -prefix -systemd-nspawn -vserver
	after sysfs
}

cgroup1_base()
{
	grep -qw cgroup /proc/filesystems || return 0
	if ! mountinfo -q /sys/fs/cgroup; then
		ebegin "Mounting cgroup filesystem"
		local opts="${cgroup_opts},mode=755,size=${rc_cgroupsize:-10m}"
		mount -n -t tmpfs -o "${opts}" cgroup_root /sys/fs/cgroup
		eend $?
	fi

	if ! mountinfo -q /sys/fs/cgroup/openrc; then
		local agent="${RC_LIBEXECDIR}/sh/cgroup-release-agent.sh"
		mkdir /sys/fs/cgroup/openrc
		mount -n -t cgroup \
			-o none,${cgroup_opts},name=openrc,release_agent="$agent" \
			openrc /sys/fs/cgroup/openrc
		printf 1 > /sys/fs/cgroup/openrc/notify_on_release
	fi
	return 0
}

cgroup1_controllers()
{
	yesno "${rc_controller_cgroups:-YES}" && [ -e /proc/cgroups ]  &&
	grep -qw cgroup /proc/filesystems || return 0
	while read -r name _ _ enabled _; do
		case "${enabled}" in
			1)	mountinfo -q "/sys/fs/cgroup/${name}" && continue
				local x
				for x in $rc_cgroup_controllers; do
				[ "${name}" = "blkio" ] && [ "${x}" = "io" ] &&
					continue 2
				[ "${name}" = "${x}" ] &&
				continue 2
				done
				mkdir "/sys/fs/cgroup/${name}"
				mount -n -t cgroup -o "${cgroup_opts},${name}" \
					"${name}" "/sys/fs/cgroup/${name}"
				yesno "${rc_cgroup_memory_use_hierarchy:-no}" &&
					[ "${name}" = memory ] &&
					echo 1 > /sys/fs/cgroup/memory/memory.use_hierarchy
				;;
		esac
	done < /proc/cgroups
	return 0
}

cgroup2_base()
{
	grep -qw cgroup2 /proc/filesystems || return 0
	local base
	base="$(cgroup2_find_path)"
	mkdir -p "${base}"
	mount -t cgroup2 none -o "${cgroup_opts},nsdelegate" "${base}" 2> /dev/null ||
		mount -t cgroup2 none -o "${cgroup_opts}" "${base}"
	return 0
}

cgroup2_controllers()
{
	grep -qw cgroup2 /proc/filesystems || return 0
	local active cgroup_path x y
	cgroup_path="$(cgroup2_find_path)"
	[ -z "${cgroup_path}" ] && return 0
	[ ! -e "${cgroup_path}/cgroup.controllers" ] && return 0
	[ ! -e "${cgroup_path}/cgroup.subtree_control" ]&& return 0
	read -r active < "${cgroup_path}/cgroup.controllers"
	for x in ${active}; do
	case "$rc_cgroup_mode" in
		unified)
			echo "+${x}"  > "${cgroup_path}/cgroup.subtree_control"
			;;
		hybrid)
			for y in ${rc_cgroup_controllers}; do
				if [ "$x" = "$y" ]; then
					echo "+${x}"  > "${cgroup_path}/cgroup.subtree_control"
				fi
			done
			;;
		esac
	done
	return 0
}

cgroups_hybrid()
{
	cgroup1_base
	cgroup2_base
	cgroup2_controllers
	cgroup1_controllers
	return 0
}

cgroups_legacy()
{
	cgroup1_base
	cgroup1_controllers
	return 0
}

cgroups_unified()
{
	cgroup2_base
	cgroup2_controllers
	return 0
}

mount_cgroups()
{
	case "${rc_cgroup_mode:-hybrid}" in
	hybrid) cgroups_hybrid ;;
	legacy) cgroups_legacy ;;
	unified) cgroups_unified ;;
	esac
	return 0
}

restorecon_cgroups()
{
	if [ -x /sbin/restorecon ]; then
		ebegin "Restoring SELinux contexts in /sys/fs/cgroup"
		restorecon -rF /sys/fs/cgroup >/dev/null 2>&1
		eend $?
	fi
	return 0
}

start()
{
	# set up kernel support for cgroups
	if [ -d /sys/fs/cgroup ]; then
		mount_cgroups
		restorecon_cgroups
	fi
	return 0
}

````

Cgroups seems to be mounted
Here is the podman info --debug

can you make sure the `rc_cgroup_mode` argument is set to `unified`?

Otherwise it looks you are mounting a group v1 hierarchy on a group v2 unified host

Inside the container I modified /etc/rc.conf and rebooted: 

cat /etc/rc.conf  | grep rc_cgroup_mode
rc_cgroup_mode="unified"

````
 cat /etc/mtab  | grep cgroup
none /sys/fs/cgroup cgroup2 rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime 0 0

````

I get:


````
podman  run --rm hello-world --cgroup_manager=cgroupfs 


WARN[0000] Failed to add conmon to cgroupfs sandbox cgroup: error creating cgroup path /libpod_parent/conmon: write /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.subtree_control: device or resource busy 
Error: OCI runtime error: the requested cgroup controller `pids` is not available

````


in the host I got:


````
 grep ^cgroup /etc/mtab
cgroup2 /sys/fs/cgroup cgroup2 rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime 0 0


````

Also inside the container it seems that podman works partially. I have a network and files created inside the cgroups folder.
````

    inet 10.88.0.1/16 brd 10.88.255.255 scope global cni-podman0
       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever

       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever

can you configure what cgroup controllers are enabled inside the container?  It seems to be missing the pids controller
Thanks. Any help on how to do that?
Ok thanks, after reading a while it seems like  LXC faults, with cgroup v 2 permissions. Let me take the issue there,

> Ok thanks, after reading a while it seems like LXC faults, with cgroup v 2 permissions. Let me take the issue there,

Hm, not really. LXC will enable and delegate all cgroup controllers that it has been given to the container. The`EBUSY` means that Podman (or runC in this case, I guess) is trying to enable controllers for delegation in a non-leaf cgroup node; something cgroup v2 forbids.

If you start the LXC container, have the init system mount v2, and look at `cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.controllers` then you know what controllers LXC had available at the time it started the container.

Thank you @brauner 
Pids controller is mounted inside Alpine 
If i do:

cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.controllers

I got

````
cpuset cpu io memory hugetlb pids rdma
````

is Podman running in the root cgroup?

I think before you can use cgroups, you need to make sure that all the existing processes are moved to a sub-cgroup, e.g. to move the init PID to a new `runtime` cgroup:

```
# mkdir /sys/fs/cgroup/runtime
# echo 1 > /sys/fs/cgroup/runtime/cgroup.procs
```

I am closing the issue since I've got no feedback on my last question.

Please reopen if the issue still persists

Hello everyone, I'm facing the same problem with my host.  

here is my environment:
- Proxmox VE 7.2-3  (cgroups v2, `lxc-pve/now 4.0.12-1`)
- Alpine v3.16 in LXC container (unprivileged, but privileged also tried and same has this issue)
- Podman v4.1.0 installed by `apk add podman`

Tried all ways above and still have this problem
```
podman-test:~# podman run alpine cat /etc/os-release
WARN[0010] Failed to add conmon to cgroupfs sandbox cgroup: error creating cgroup path /libpod_parent/conmon: write /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.subtree_control: device or resource busy 
Error: OCI runtime error: crun: the requested cgroup controller `pids` is not available
```
does anyway to solve?

Additionally, I have made the following attempts:
- Install docker on same container, there are so many error logs when running `service docker start`, but seems docker works fine.
```
podman-test:~# service docker start

- Create a Ubuntu LXC container on same host and install podman 3.4.4 (using `apt install podman`), works fine
could we reopen this issue?
got works by setting `pids_limit=0` according this issue: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/9410#issuecomment-785840320
Great.  We don't like to reopen issues, when this would probably been better as a discussion. Usually not much upstream podman can do to get it to run in a confined environment like a container.
I am sorry @dev-techmoe, I just did not followed because I could not make it work and moved to a VM instead.
